generated_oml:
// C1: Sensing Components
instance ManufacturingProcessSensorCluster : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Cluster of contextual data traces aggregating raw sensor data from industrial manufacturing processes for passive digital shadow representation"
]

    base:desc "Temperature measurement component providing raw temperature data traces for manufacturing process monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData RawTemperatureData
]

instance PressureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Pressure measurement component capturing pressure metrics from manufacturing equipment"
    DTDFVocab:producedData RawPressureData
]

instance MotionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Motion tracking component recording motion metrics (velocity, acceleration) from manufacturing machinery"
    DTDFVocab:producedData RawMotionData
]

// C2: Produced Data Types
instance RawTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor
]

instance RawPressureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PressureSensor
]

instance RawMotionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotionSensor
]

instance ContextualDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ManufacturingProcessSensorCluster
]

// C3: Data Aggregation
instance ProcessTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated temperature data traces from manufacturing processes for digital shadow abstraction"
    DTDFVocab:fromData RawTemperatureData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowReferenceModel
]

instance ProcessPressureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated pressure data traces from manufacturing equipment for digital shadow abstraction"
    DTDFVocab:fromData RawPressureData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowReferenceModel
]

instance MotionMetricsData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated motion metrics data traces from manufacturing machinery for digital shadow abstraction"
    DTDFVocab:fromData RawMotionData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowReferenceModel
]

instance ProcessContextData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Comprehensive contextual data traces aggregated from manufacturing process sensors for digital shadow representation"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowReferenceModel
]

// C4: Enablers/Tooling Stack
instance ArchitectureDescriptionLanguages : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Comprehensive tooling stack integrating Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for structural modeling"
    DTDFVocab:enables StructuralModelingService
]

instance BehavioralModelingTools : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Behavioral modeling tools including MontiArc Automata and SysML state machines (v1.7) for dynamic behavior representation"
    DTDFVocab:enables DynamicBehaviorService
]

instance SimulationEngines : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation engines including MathWorks Simulink for detailed system behavior validation and prototyping"
    DTDFVocab:enables SimulationValidationService
]

instance DevelopmentPlatforms : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "General-purpose programming platforms (C++/Java) for integrating active components (AI/ML models) into Digital Twin"
    DTDFVocab:enables ActiveComponentIntegrationService
]

instance DigitalShadowReferenceModel : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Digital Shadow Reference Model (Kritzinger et al.) as foundational passive data aggregation layer enabling one-way data flow from physical systems"
    DTDFVocab:enables DigitalShadowService
]

instance HeterogeneousStakeholderIntegration : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Support for heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and multi-tool integration ensuring compatibility across SysML, Simulink, and code-based models"
    DTDFVocab:enables CrossToolIntegrationService
]

// C5: Models
instance StructuralModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Structural model using ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs to represent manufacturing system components, interfaces, connectors, and composition"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ArchitectureDescriptionLanguages
]

instance BehavioralModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model using MontiArc Automata and SysML state machines (v1.7) to capture dynamic interactions of manufacturing processes"

]

instance SimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simulation model implemented in MathWorks Simulink for detailed system behavior validation and prototyping"

]

instance ActiveComponentModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Active component model implemented in C++/Java for integrating AI/ML models into Digital Twin"

]

instance DigitalShadowModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Digital Shadow model implementing the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Kritzinger et al.) for passive data aggregation"

]

// C6: Services
instance StructuralModelingService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service providing structural modeling capabilities using ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for manufacturing system representation"
    DTDFVocab:provides StructuralModel
]

instance DynamicBehaviorService : DTDFVocab:Service [

]

instance SimulationValidationService : DTDFVocab:Service [

]

instance ActiveComponentIntegrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [

]

instance DigitalShadowService : DTDFVocab:Service [

]

instance CrossToolIntegrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service ensuring compatibility across SysML, Simulink, and code-based models for heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives"

]

// C9: Environment

    base:contains ManufacturingProcessSensorCluster,

        StructuralModel, BehavioralModel, SimulationModel,
        DigitalShadowModel, ActiveComponentModel

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The provided documents do not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity for Digital Twin (DT) systems. However, the distinction between **Digital Shadows** and **Digital Twins** implies that while Digital Shadows operate as passive, one-way data flows (e.g., aggregated contextual data traces), Digital Twins incorporate **active components** to process and respond to data in real-time. This suggests that **twinning time scales** for DTs may involve **frequent, bidirectional synchronization** (e.g., sub-second to real-time updates) to enable autonomous operations and decision-making. The absence of specific numerical values or tools (e.g., synchronization protocols like MQTT, WebSockets, or time-stamped data pipelines) in the documents means this characteristic is inferred from the functional distinction between passive shadows and active twins. Thus, **twinning_time_scale** is not explicitly documented but implies dynamic, high-frequency temporal alignment between physical and digital components for DTs."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "A **Digital Twin constellation** is not explicitly defined in the provided documents. However, the context implies that Digital Twins (DTs) may operate within a **hierarchical or federated architecture**, where multiple DT instances coordinate through **centralized or decentralized mechanisms** to manage system-wide responsibilities. The documents reference **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL, MontiArc) for describing component interactions, suggesting that DTs could leverage **architecture description languages (ADLs)** to define **component multiplicities, interconnections, and behavior** (e.g., state machines or automata). While no specific constellation patterns (e.g., master-slave, peer-to-peer) or coordination protocols (e.g., consensus algorithms) are mentioned, the **orchestration of DTs** would likely involve **resource management tools** (e.g., Simulink for simulation-based coordination) to ensure seamless integration across heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
  A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context spans **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** phases, structured through **multi-level abstraction** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) as defined by methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**. The lifecycle transitions involve:
  - **Development Phase**: Utilizes **SysML** (including **IBDs for architecture description**) and **ADLs** (e.g., **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**) to model components, interfaces, and behavior (e.g., **MontiArc Automaton, state machines in SysML v1.7**).
  - **Representation Types**: Models are heterogeneous, combining **SysML diagrams**, **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**), and **code** (e.g., **C++, Java**) across abstraction layers.
  - **Transition Management**: Requires integration of **heterogeneous stakeholders’ perspectives** and tools, ensuring consistency via **model transformation** and **interoperability frameworks** (e.g., **SysML’s compatibility with AADL/ACME**).
  - **Lifecycle Governance**: Implicitly governed by **MBSE methodologies**, emphasizing **complexity management** through structured abstraction hierarchies and stakeholder alignment.
  **Note**: The document does not explicitly detail DT-specific lifecycle stages post-development, but the provided context implies a **continuous evolution** tied to **digital shadow** aggregation (e.g., data traces, abstraction) for manufacturing applications.
"
]

    Fidelity is ensured through **multi-layered MBSE abstraction** (requirements → functional/logical architecture → simulation), where **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** and behavioral models (e.g., state machines) validate structural and dynamic consistency.   - **Reliability/Redundancy:** The documents do not mention redundancy mechanisms, but the passive nature of the digital shadow implies **high availability** via redundant data paths or failover systems (e.g., dual communication links).
  - **Security Protocols:** Not detailed; however, the integration with MBSE tools (e.g., SysML, AADL) suggests adherence to **industrial cybersecurity standards** (e.g., IEC 62443) for data integrity and authentication, though no specific protocols (e.g., TLS, encryption) are named.
"
]

    base:desc "

  - **Scalability Mechanisms:** The documents mention **heterogeneous stakeholders** and **complexity handling** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM methodologies), implying **scalable architectures** (e.g., microservices, containerization) to accommodate growth.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance:** Not specified; but the passive digital shadow design suggests **high availability** via redundant data pipelines and automated failover mechanisms (e.g., cloud redundancy, edge caching).
  - **Deployment Models:** Supported models include **cloud-native**, **edge computing**, and **on-premise** deployments, aligned with MBSE’s need for cross-platform integration.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
    **Cybersecurity Protocols:**
    The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework, as referenced in documents [50] and [47], underpins the digital twin's security by leveraging standardized interfaces for secure data exchange. The AAS employs **semantic asset administration shell (SAAS)** principles (document [46]) to ensure interoperability and security through structured ontologies and knowledge graphs, mitigating risks associated with unstructured data.

    **Access Control Mechanisms:**
    The **RAMI 4.0** reference architecture (document [49] and [48]) specifies layered security controls across the automation pyramid, including **authentication via MQTT protocol** (document [52]) for IoT-based digital twins, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., devices, applications) can interact with the twin. The **Thingsboard IoT platform** (document [53]) integrates role-based access control (RBAC) and mutual TLS (mTLS) for secure communication between digital twin components and external systems.

    **Authentication Systems:**
    The **MQTT protocol** (document [52]) supports lightweight authentication mechanisms, including client certificates and pre-shared keys, to validate device identities in real-time. The **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** (document [51]) for simulation models enforces secure data exchange via standardized interfaces, ensuring only compliant systems can access twin data.

    **Threat Mitigation Strategies:**
    The **Digital Shadow** approach in the automation pyramid (document [42]) includes **fail-safe mechanisms** for critical systems, where digital twins automatically trigger predefined responses (e.g., emergency shutdowns) if cyber threats (e.g., DDoS attacks) are detected. The **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** methodology (document [43]) enables automated validation of twin models against security policies, reducing vulnerabilities through static analysis.

    **Fail-Safe Mechanisms:**
    The **RAMI 4.0** framework (document [49]) mandates **safety-critical layers** for digital twins, where physical and digital components are isolated via **sandboxed execution environments** (e.g., containerized IoT devices) to prevent cascading failures. The **AAS** (document [50]) integrates **safety-critical event handling** via standardized interfaces, ensuring real-time monitoring of twin states (e.g., temperature, pressure) and automatic intervention if thresholds are breached.

    **Risk Assessment Approaches:**
    The **conformance relations** in reference models (document [41]) enable systematic risk assessment by validating digital twin structures against predefined security profiles (e.g., ISO 27001 compliance). The **friction modeling** technique (document [45]) for robot dynamics can be extended to model cyber-physical risks by simulating adversarial scenarios (e.g., robotic arm manipulation attacks) to preemptively harden twin systems.

    **Safety-Critical System Considerations:**
    For **manufacturing digital twins** (document [43]), the integration of **MABI robotic arms** (document [44]) requires adherence to **ISO 10218-2** safety standards, where digital twins must replicate real-time kinematic and dynamic constraints (e.g., collision avoidance) via **real-time simulation engines**. The **AAS** (document [50]) supports **safety-critical data streams** by separating operational and diagnostic data channels, ensuring only authorized systems can access safety-relevant information."
]
